method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is rendered in English as “western island”.[5] The name reflects the island’s position west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Islands.[1][5] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was formerly known as Rosario Island, after the vessel, before it was renamed Nishinoshima.[1]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Islands.[4] It lies on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge, the volcanic front of the Izu–Bonin arc, which trends roughly north–south parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanism in the region is caused by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4]

The island forms the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[5] The volcanic edifice includes the main peak exposed above sea level, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4] Nishinoshima has generally been described as an andesitic volcanic island, with ejecta from recorded activity having SiO₂ contents of 51.8–60.1 wt%.[5]

The first historically recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island.[5] That eruption began with submarine phreatomagmatic explosions, formed new islands by September 1973, and produced volcanic blocks and lava flows from multiple craters.[5] Although erosion removed much of the new volcanic edifices, reworked deposits connected the old and new islands, and by 1982 deposit movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[5]

From 2013 to 2020, Nishinoshima underwent a multi-episode eruptive sequence monitored mainly by satellites, aircraft, and research vessels because of its remote location.[4] The 2013–2020 activity has been divided into four episodes: a two-year Episode 1 in 2013–2015, a 120-day Episode 2 in 2017, a roughly week-long Episode 3, and a 270-day Episode 4 in 2019–2020.[3] Episode 4 initially featured Strombolian activity with lava effusion, then lava fountains, and finally ash emission likely generated by phreatomagmatic eruptions.[4]

Petrological analysis of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption showed that it involved basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas.[3] The andesites resemble those erupted at Nishinoshima since 1973, and their primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] The 2020 basalts are comparable to older basalts from surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma may have been generated throughout the volcano’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is ecologically significant as a highly disturbed oceanic island within the Ogasawara Islands, whose isolation has made the wider archipelago an important area for studying colonization, speciation and adaptive radiation.[1][5] The Ogasawara Islands support many endemic and rare species, particularly among land snails, insects and vascular plants, but Nishinoshima differs from older islands in that its habitats are repeatedly altered by eruptions and coastal erosion.[1][5] These disturbances make the island a natural site for observing primary succession, including the arrival and establishment of plants on newly formed volcanic surfaces.[2]

Vegetation on Nishinoshima has been vulnerable to repeated volcanic disturbance, with eruptions reported to destroy much of the island’s plant life.[4] A population of common purslane, *Portulaca oleracea*, was collected by researchers before a later eruption and was subsequently reported to have disappeared from the island after the 2019 eruptive activity.[4] Earlier studies also examined the ecological characteristics and invasion of purslane and other plants on Nishinoshima’s newly formed land, reflecting the island’s role in research on plant colonization.[2]

Because Nishinoshima is remote and frequently reshaped, its terrestrial ecosystem is comparatively young and unstable, while surrounding marine habitats are also subject to continuing formation and change.[1][3] Scientific monitoring of the island has included volcanic activity, species colonization and the development of marine ecosystems such as coral communities around new landforms.[3] Within the broader Ogasawara World Heritage context, Nishinoshima contributes to the archipelago’s value as a living laboratory for ecological succession and evolution on isolated oceanic islands.[3][5]
